text: The robotic instruments are visible within the surgical field, with the spleen located in the upper right corner of the screen and the stomach positioned on the left side.

The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the omentum, a fatty layer connecting the stomach to the transverse colon and spleen. Visible within the omentum are the short gastric vessels, which the surgeon begins to divide.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, an energy device, to cauterize and divide the short gastric vessels within the omentum, effectively sealing them to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the stomach from the spleen, which are closely attached at the superior pole of the spleen. The stomach is visible on the left side of the screen, while the spleen is on the right.

The surgeon completes the division of the final attachment between the stomach and spleen, causing the stomach to fall to the left side of the screen. The spleen remains attached to the diaphragm at the superior pole, where the surgeon proceeds to divide the attachments, gradually mobilizing the spleen from the diaphragm.

The surgeon completes the dissection at the superior pole of the spleen and begins working down towards the inferior pole. Visible attachments between the splenic flexure of the transverse colon and the abdominal wall are observed.

The surgeon takes down the short gastric vessels within the omentum to improve access and visibility of the inferior pole and lateral portion of the spleen, where attachments to the lateral abdominal wall are visible.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the splenocolic ligament at the inferior pole of the spleen. The ligament is visibly separated without any named vessels present, ensuring safety through cauterization.

The surgeon retracts the spleen medially towards the middle of the abdomen, creating tension to visualize the attachments between the lateral portion of the spleen and the abdominal wall. The surgeon proceeds to divide these attachments to further mobilize the spleen.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery device to mobilize the spleen, carefully burning through tissue in an avascular plane to avoid smaller blood vessels.
The surgeon lifts the left lateral segment of the liver, visible on the left side of the screen, to gain access to the spleen located beneath.

The surgeon lifts the spleen completely, revealing the remaining attachments at the hilum. Using a stapler, the surgeon divides the splenic artery and vein, which are visible running behind the tail of the pancreas. Care is taken to avoid the tail of the pancreas while stapling the vessels.
The surgeon completes the division of the splenic hilum using multiple firings of the stapler, fully detaching the spleen. The spleen is then placed into a retrieval bag for removal.
The surgeon places the freed spleen into a retrieval bag using robotic instruments, preparing to remove it from the abdominal cavity.
